claim: Rats learned to recognize two categories of orientation: 0° ± 45° (''horizontal'') and 90° ± 45° (''vertical'').
source: Each trial required a visual (V), a tactile (T), or a visual-tactile (VT) discrimination; VT performance was better than that predicted by optimal linear combination of V and T signals, indicating synergy between sensory channels.
evidence: This is stated directly in the abstract and methods describing the experimental design.

claim: VT performance was better than that predicted by optimal linear combination of V and T signals, indicating synergy between sensory channels.
source: Each trial required a visual (V), a tactile (T), or a visual-tactile (VT) discrimination; VT performance was better than that predicted by optimal linear combination of V and T signals, indicating synergy between sensory channels.
evidence: This claim is supported by behavioral data comparing observed VT performance to Bayesian model predictions.

claim: PPC carried both graded information about object orientation and categorical information about the rat's upcoming choice.
source: We examined posterior parietal cortex (PPC) and uncovered key neuronal correlates of the behavioral findings: PPC carried both graded information about object orientation and categorical information about the rat's upcoming choice
evidence: This is supported by neuronal recordings and information theoretic analysis of PPC activity.

claim: Single neurons in PPC exhibited identical responses under the three modality conditions (V, T, and VT).
source: single neurons exhibited identical responses under the three modality conditions.
evidence: This is demonstrated through analysis of neuronal firing rates across modality conditions showing high correlation (R = 0.96-0.99).

claim: A linear classifier of neuronal population firing replicated the behavioral findings.
source: Finally, a linear classifier of neuronal population firing replicated the behavioral findings.
evidence: Linear discriminant analysis on population activity achieved >90% accuracy in predicting rat choices.

claim: PPC is involved in the supramodal processing of shape.
source: Taken together, these findings suggest that PPC is involved in the supramodal processing of shape.
evidence: This conclusion is drawn from the combination of behavioral and neurophysiological findings showing modality-invariant representations.

claim: Humans, non-human primates, and rodents demonstrate heightened accuracy of perceptual judgements when multiple cues are combined.
source: In psychophysical studies, humans, non-human primates, and rodents demonstrate heightened accuracy of perceptual judgements when multiple cues are combined, and in some studies the increase in accuracy approximates that of a statistically optimal observer (Alais and Burr, 2004; Battaglia et al., 2003; Ernst and Banks, 2002; Fetsch et al., 2009; Guo et al., 2014; Jacobs, 1999; Raposo et al., 2012).
evidence: This claim is supported by citations to previous psychophysical studies.

claim: PPC is situated between primary somatosensory and visual cortices and is a target of both.
source: Since object recognition is usually attributed to neocortical processing, here we examine the posterior parietal cortex (PPC), situated between primary somatosensory and visual cortices and a target of both (Akers and Killackey, 1978; Krubitzer, 1995; Reep et al., 1994; Whitlock et al., 2008; Wilber et al., 2014).
evidence: This anatomical claim is supported by citations to neuroanatomical studies.

claim: PPC is a good candidate for visual-tactile convergence.
source: PPC is a good candidate for visual-tactile convergence (Olcese et al., 2013), although in rodents the degree of multisensory convergence within it is debated (Erlich et al., 2015; Harvey et al., 2012; Licata et al., 2017; Lippert et al., 2013; Raposo et al., 2014).
evidence: This claim is supported by citation to Olcese et al., 2013, while acknowledging debate in the field.

claim: The degree of multisensory convergence within PPC in rodents is debated.
source: PPC is a good candidate for visual-tactile convergence (Olcese et al., 2013), although in rodents the degree of multisensory convergence within it is debated (Erlich et al., 2015; Harvey et al., 2012; Licata et al., 2017; Lippert et al., 2013; Raposo et al., 2014).
evidence: This acknowledges ongoing scientific debate supported by multiple citations.

claim: The discriminandum had a circular boundary (98 mm diameter) and raised parallel bars.
source: The discriminandum, pictured in Figure 1A, had a circular boundary (98 mm diameter) and raised parallel bars
evidence: This describes the physical stimulus used in the experiment, shown in Figure 1A.

claim: The raised parallel bars had width and depth of 3.5 mm, alternately colored white and black.
source: (width and depth of 3.5 mm), alternately colored white and black.
evidence: This provides specific measurements of the stimulus features.

claim: Rats were trained to categorize orientations in the range of 0°–45° as horizontal, and orientations in the range of 45°–90° as vertical.
source: Rats were trained to encounter the object to judge its orientation on each trial, categorizing orientations in the range of 0°–45° as horizontal, and orientations in the range of 45°–90° as vertical (Figure 1B; also see STAR Methods and Figure S1).
evidence: This describes the behavioral task design.

claim: The three stimulus conditions (V, T, and VT) were randomly interleaved with 32% likelihood per trial.
source: The three stimulus conditions (V, T, and VT) were randomly interleaved with 32% likelihood per trial.
evidence: This describes the experimental design for trial presentation.

claim: The remaining 4% were control trials (neither visual nor tactile access to the stimulus).
source: The remaining 4% were control trials (neither visual nor tactile access to the stimulus).
evidence: This describes control trials in the experimental design.

claim: Rat 3 performed better in the T condition.
source: Figure 2A reveals that rats differed in their modality-specific acuity: rat 3 performed better in the T condition (left), while rats 12 and 1 performed better in the V condition (middle and right).
evidence: This is shown in Figure 2A with psychometric curves for individual rats.

claim: Rats 12 and 1 performed better in the V condition.
source: Figure 2A reveals that rats differed in their modality-specific acuity: rat 3 performed better in the T condition (left), while rats 12 and 1 performed better in the V condition (middle and right).
evidence: This is shown in Figure 2A with psychometric curves for individual rats.

claim: All three example rats performed better in the VT condition than in V or T alone.
source: All three example rats performed better in the VT condition than in V or T alone (in this figure, and throughout the report, green traces correspond to the T condition, blue to V, and red to VT).
evidence: This is demonstrated in Figure 2A showing psychometric curves.

claim: The boost in performance in the VT condition indicates that, when both modalities were available, rats did not select and rely upon the single, preferred channel.
source: The boost in performance in the VT condition indicates that, when both modalities were available, rats did not select and rely upon the single, preferred channel; instead, they benefited from the access to both channels of sensory input (Figure S2B).
evidence: This interpretation is based on the observed performance improvement in VT trials.

claim: Rats showed, on average, good orientation acuity using their whiskers and snout (T).
source: Rats showed, on average, good orientation acuity using their whiskers and snout (T) and slightly better performance using vision (V).
evidence: This is based on analysis of psychometric curves across 12 rats.

claim: Rats showed slightly better performance using vision (V) than tactile (T).
source: Rats showed, on average, good orientation acuity using their whiskers and snout (T) and slightly better performance using vision (V).
evidence: This is based on analysis of psychometric curves across 12 rats.

claim: Performance was markedly superior in the VT condition, indicating multisensory enhancement.
source: Performance was markedly superior in the VT condition, indicating multisensory enhancement, consistent with Figure 2A.
evidence: This is demonstrated by comparing psychometric curves across modality conditions.

claim: The decrease in σ in the VT versus V and T conditions was significant for all rats (p < 0.001).
source: Individual rats performed best on bimodal trials, independently of the individual's preferred modality: the decrease in s in the VT versus V and T conditions was significant for all rats (Figures S2A and S2B, bootstrap test, p < 0.001).
evidence: This statistical result is based on bootstrap testing of psychometric curve parameters.

claim: In the VT condition, rats showed reduced lapse rates compared to V and T.
source: In the VT condition, rats also showed reduced lapse rates compared to V and T (i.e., fewer errors on ''easy'' trials, those near the 0° and 90° orientations, p = 0.000 for all conditions in average curve; Figure S2A, bootstrap test)
evidence: This is demonstrated through statistical analysis of psychometric curve parameters.

claim: Rats showed a more accurate detection of the horizontal and vertical boundary in VT condition, assessed by the point of subjective equality (PSE closer to 45°, p < 0.02 for V and T in average curve compared to VT).
source: and a more accurate detection of the horizontal and vertical boundary, assessed by the point of subjective equality (PSE closer to 45°, p < 0.02 for V and T in average curve compared to VT; Figure S2A, bootstrap test).
evidence: This is based on statistical analysis of the PSE parameter from psychometric curves.

claim: At 45°, performance can be no better than chance; thus, multisensory integration provided no benefit.
source: At 45°, performance can be no better than chance; thus, multisensory integration provided no benefit.
evidence: This is a logical consequence of the task design where 45° is the category boundary.

claim: At the cardinal orientations, 0° and 90°, performance in V and T conditions was high, so that multisensory integration could offer limited benefit.
source: At the cardinal orientations, 0° and 90°, performance in V and T conditions was high, so that multisensory integration could offer limited benefit.
evidence: This is based on the observed ceiling effect in performance at cardinal orientations.

claim: At the intermediate orientations, 20°–35° and 55°–70°, the benefit obtained by combining information from the two modalities was greatest.
source: At the intermediate orientations, 20°–35° and 55°–70°, the benefit obtained by combining information from the two modalities was greatest.
evidence: This is shown in the inset of Figure 2B plotting benefit as a function of orientation.

claim: In ten rats who were well trained with stimuli in the range of 0°–90°, trials with stimulus orientations ranging from 90°–135° and −45° to 0° were introduced.
source: To distinguish between the possibilities, in ten rats who were well trained with stimuli in the range of 0°–90°, we introduced trials with stimulus orientations ranging from 90°–135° and −45° to 0° (Figure 1B, stippled sectors) interleaved with the familiar 0°–90° trials in the same session.
evidence: This describes a generalization test conducted in the experiment.

claim: In the first session with novel orientations, each rat performed equally well on the new versus the familiar stimuli (bootstrap test, all rats p > 0.29).
source: In the first such session, each rat performed equally well on the new versus the familiar stimuli (bootstrap test, all rats p > 0.29; Figure S2C).
evidence: This statistical result demonstrates immediate generalization to novel orientations.

claim: Rats seemed to solve the task by measuring the difference between the encountered orientation and the cardinal orientations of ''horizontal'' and ''vertical.''
source: Thus, rats seemed to solve the task by measuring the difference between the encountered orientation and the cardinal orientations of ''horizontal'' and ''vertical.''
evidence: This interpretation is based on the immediate generalization to novel orientations.

claim: Rats were able to generalize this categorization rule to new stimuli upon the first instance.
source: They were able to generalize this categorization rule to new stimuli upon the first instance, consistent with the ability of rats to generalize visual object recognition to novel views (Tafazoli et al., 2012).
evidence: This is demonstrated by the generalization test and supported by citation to previous work.

claim: 9 out of 12 rats combined V and T signals in a supralinear manner.
source: To illustrate all rats together, Figure 2C plots the measured threshold on VT trials versus the threshold predicted by linear combination of V and T signals: 9 out of 12 rats combined V and T signals in a supralinear manner (similar to rat 1 in Figure 2A), 2 in a linear manner (similar to rat 3 in Figure 2A), and 1 sublinearly (rat 12 in Figure 2A).
evidence: This is based on comparison of measured VT thresholds to Bayesian model predictions.

claim: 2 rats combined V and T signals in a linear manner.
source: To illustrate all rats together, Figure 2C plots the measured threshold on VT trials versus the threshold predicted by linear combination of V and T signals: 9 out of 12 rats combined V and T signals in a supralinear manner (similar to rat 1 in Figure 2A), 2 in a linear manner (similar to rat 3 in Figure 2A), and 1 sublinearly (rat 12 in Figure 2A).
evidence: This is based on comparison of measured VT thresholds to Bayesian model predictions.

claim: 1 rat combined V and T signals sublinearly.
source: To illustrate all rats together, Figure 2C plots the measured threshold on VT trials versus the threshold predicted by linear combination of V and T signals: 9 out of 12 rats combined V and T signals in a supralinear manner (similar to rat 1 in Figure 2A), 2 in a linear manner (similar to rat 3 in Figure 2A), and 1 sublinearly (rat 12 in Figure 2A).
evidence: This is based on comparison of measured VT thresholds to Bayesian model predictions.

claim: 8 out of 12 rats combined V and T signals in a strongly supralinear manner based on mutual information analysis.
source: Figure 2D shows that 8 out of 12 rats combined V and T signals in a strongly supralinear manner, 2 rats close to supralinear (one error bar overlies the diagonal), 1 in a linear manner (both error bars overlie the diagonal), and 1 in a sublinear manner.
evidence: This is based on mutual information analysis comparing VT information to sum of V and T information.

claim: Two analyses—Bayesian prediction of underlying threshold and MI—both suggest that in the majority of rats the signals carried by the two sensory channels were not merged independently but were combined in a synergistic manner.
source: In summary, two analyses—Bayesian prediction of underlying threshold and MI—both suggest that in the majority of rats the signals carried by the two sensory channels were not merged independently but were combined in a synergistic manner (the two measures are correlated, linear regression R = 0.6; Figure S2D).
evidence: This conclusion is based on convergent results from two independent analytical approaches.

claim: The two measures (Bayesian threshold and MI) are correlated, linear regression R = 0.6.
source: In summary, two analyses—Bayesian prediction of underlying threshold and MI—both suggest that in the majority of rats the signals carried by the two sensory channels were not merged independently but were combined in a synergistic manner (the two measures are correlated, linear regression R = 0.6; Figure S2D).
evidence: This statistical relationship is shown in Figure S2D.

claim: In the VT condition, the distribution of PSE was closer to the 45° line and curve slope was greater.
source: In the VT condition, the distribution of PSE was closer to the 45° line and curve slope was greater; average values are in the black circles.
evidence: This is shown in Figure 2E plotting PSE and psychometric curve slope.

claim: For the block design, measured σVT values remained close to or better than Bayesian optimal estimates.
source: For this block design, measured sVT values remained close to or better than Bayesian optimal estimates (Figure 2F), indicating that supralinear summation in the interleaved sessions was not well explained by enhanced motivation on VT trials.
evidence: This is shown in Figure 2F comparing measured to predicted thresholds in block design.

claim: Supralinear summation in the interleaved sessions was not well explained by enhanced motivation on VT trials.
source: For this block design, measured sVT values remained close to or better than Bayesian optimal estimates (Figure 2F), indicating that supralinear summation in the interleaved sessions was not well explained by enhanced motivation on VT trials.
evidence: This conclusion is based on the persistence of supralinear performance in block design where motivation confounds are minimized.

claim: Response times were 435–734 ms (interquartile range).
source: Considering all sensory conditions and stimulus orientations, response times were 435–734 ms (interquartile range; Figure S2E).
evidence: This is a direct measurement from behavioral data.

claim: Response times were longer for orientations close to the category boundary, 45°.
source: With trials sorted according to stimulus orientation, it becomes apparent that response times were longer for orientations close to the category boundary, 45° (Figure 2G).
evidence: This is shown in Figure 2G plotting response time as a function of orientation.

claim: The neuron had a stable firing rate of about 2 spikes/s as the rat awaited the trial onset.
source: The neuron had a stable firing rate of about 2 spikes/s as the rat awaited the trial onset.
evidence: This describes the baseline firing rate of an example neuron shown in Figure 3B.

claim: About 200 ms after trial onset, the neuron's firing rate ramped upward and remained at about 8 spikes/s until 1 s after trial onset.
source: About 200 ms after trial onset, the neuron's firing rate ramped upward and remained at about 8 spikes/s until 1 s after trial onset.
evidence: This describes the response dynamics of an example neuron shown in Figure 3B.

claim: Similar temporal profiles characterized V, T, and VT trials for the example neuron.
source: Similar temporal profiles characterized V, T, and VT trials.
evidence: This describes the modality-invariant response of an example neuron shown in Figure 3B.

claim: The first example neuron's firing rate was modulated by stimulus orientation, firing at a progressively higher rate as angle increased from 0° to 90°.
source: The angular tuning curves (right plots, separated by modality, based on neuronal data in a 300 ms window preceding the reward spout lick) highlight that the two neurons' firing rates were modulated by stimulus orientation, the first firing at a progressively higher rate as angle increased from 0° to 90°
evidence: This describes the orientation tuning of an example neuron shown in Figure 3C.

claim: The second example neuron's firing rate was modulated by stimulus orientation, firing at a progressively lower rate from 0° to 90°.
source: and the second firing at a progressively lower rate from 0° to 90°.
evidence: This describes the orientation tuning of an example neuron shown in Figure 3C.

claim: Two neurons carried a large signal about stimulus category, with firing rates deviating according to the upcoming action (left or right).
source: By contrast, Figure 3D shows two neurons that carried a large signal about stimulus category: firing rates deviated according to the upcoming action (left or right).
evidence: This describes category-selective neurons shown in Figure 3D.

claim: The category-selective neurons were poorly modulated by stimulus orientation within a choice category.
source: The angular tuning curves indicate that they were poorly modulated by stimulus orientation within a choice category.
evidence: This describes the tuning properties of category-selective neurons shown in Figure 3D.

claim: Each point in Figure 4A represents the average firing rates of one neuron (total, 622 neurons) in a 300 ms window preceding the reward spout lick in V, T, and VT trials, separately.
source: In Figure 4A, each point represents the average firing rates of one neuron (total, 622 neurons) in a 300 ms window preceding the reward spout lick in V, T, and VT trials, separately.
evidence: This describes the data shown in Figure 4A analyzing modality invariance.

claim: The proximity of nearly all points to the diagonal line attests to the supramodal character of these neurons.
source: The proximity of nearly all points to the diagonal line attests to the supramodal character of these neurons: a given neuron's overall engagement in the task was nearly equal under the three modality conditions.
evidence: This interpretation is based on the distribution of points in Figure 4A.

claim: The Pearson linear correlation coefficient, R, between firing rate on V and T trials ranged from 0.96 to 0.99 across the six stimulus orientations.
source: The independence of firing rate on modality held across the six stimulus orientations; the Pearson linear correlation coefficient, R, between firing rate on V and T trials ranged from 0.96 to 0.99.
evidence: This statistical result is shown in Figure 4B demonstrating modality invariance.

claim: Just 5% (30 out of 622) of PPC neurons were selective (p < 0.01; permutation test) for stimulus modality.
source: Just 5% (30 out of 622) were selective (p < 0.01; permutation test) for stimulus modality, and the analysis does not point to an overall preference for either modality among these 30 neurons.
evidence: This is based on ROC analysis of modality selectivity shown in Figure 4D.

claim: Over 50% of neurons (318 out of 622) were significantly selective (p < 0.01; permutation test) for the upcoming choice of the rat.
source: Figure 4D, lower histogram, shows the distribution of category selectivity for the same population of neurons; over 50% of neurons (318 out of 622) were significantly selective (p < 0.01; permutation test) for the upcoming choice of the rat.
evidence: This is based on ROC analysis of category selectivity shown in Figure 4D.

claim: A greater number of neurons (219 out of 318 neurons) was selective for turns to the right (index < 0) than to the left (index > 0), suggesting overall opposite-side preference in left PPC.
source: A greater number of neurons (219 out of 318 neurons) was selective for turns to the right (index < 0) than to the left (index > 0), suggesting overall opposite-side preference in left PPC.
evidence: This is based on analysis of the distribution of category selectivity indices.

claim: This population of neurons did not appear to be informative about the modality of the stimulus but instead generated a robust signal about stimulus properties—orientation and the choice category associated with that orientation—irrespective of modality.
source: In sum, this population of neurons did not appear to be informative about the modality of the stimulus but instead generated a robust signal about stimulus properties—orientation and the choice category associated with that orientation—irrespective of modality (also see Figure S4).
evidence: This conclusion is based on the selectivity analysis showing low modality selectivity but high category selectivity.

claim: About 50% of neurons (315 out 622, p < 0.01) carried significant information about category conditional on angle.
source: About 50% of neurons (315 out 622, p < 0.01) carried significant information about category conditional on angle (blue triangles and blue histogram projection)
evidence: This is based on conditional mutual information analysis shown in Figure 5.

claim: About 45% of neurons (284 out of 622, p < 0.01) carried significant information about stimulus angle conditional upon perceived category.
source: About 45% of neurons (284 out of 622, p < 0.01) carried significant information about stimulus angle conditional upon perceived category (brick-colored triangles and histogram projection).
evidence: This is based on conditional mutual information analysis shown in Figure 5.

claim: About 38% of neurons (239 out of 622, p < 0.01, bootstrap test) carried significant conditional information about both orientation and choice.
source: About 38% of neurons (239 out of 622, p < 0.01, bootstrap test) carried significant conditional information about both orientation and choice (stars and ochre-colored histograms and squares).
evidence: This is based on conditional mutual information analysis shown in Figure 5.

claim: 78% of neurons that carried information about both angle and category were clustered between the bands (R±0.05 bits of information).
source: the fact that 78% of neurons that carried information about both angle and category were clustered between the bands indicates that PPC neurons tended to express both stimulus coding and category coding.
evidence: This is based on analysis of the distribution in Figure 5.

claim: The simultaneous representation of both task properties suggests that PPC is involved in a supramodal stimulus-to-category transformation.
source: The simultaneous representation of both task properties suggests that PPC is involved in a supramodal stimulus-to-category transformation.
evidence: This conclusion is based on the finding that neurons carry both orientation and category information.

claim: The classifier was able to determine a boundary that could separate the population's activity into two states associated with the rat's two choices.
source: As schematically illustrated for one typical session (Figure 6A), the classifier was able to determine a boundary that could separate the population's activity into two states associated with the rat's two choices.
evidence: This describes the linear discriminant analysis shown in Figure 6A.

claim: About 1 s before the response (i.e., before trial onset), the decoder's performance was around 50%.
source: About 1 s before the response (i.e., before trial onset), the decoder's performance was around 50%.
evidence: This is shown in Figure 6B plotting decoder performance over time.

claim: This suggests that the PPC population did not manifest a choice bias before encountering the stimulus.
source: This suggests that the PPC population did not manifest a choice bias before encountering the stimulus.
evidence: This interpretation is based on the chance-level decoder performance before trial onset.

claim: Performance gradually increased to >90% on average by around 250 ms before the response lick.
source: Performance gradually increased to >90% on average by around 250 ms before the response lick.
evidence: This is shown in Figure 6B plotting decoder performance over time.

claim: Peak decoder performance measured in 5 rats ranged from 91% to 99% (9 sessions), with 1 session at 70% (p < 0.001, permutation test).
source: Peak decoder performance measured in 5 rats ranged from 91% to 99% (9 sessions), with 1 session at 70% (p < 0.001, permutation test).
evidence: This statistical result is based on linear discriminant analysis across recording sessions.

claim: Almost all neuronal weights were non-zero in each session, indicating that every neuron in the population contributed to the classifier's performance.
source: To make sure the decoder's performance did not result from the activity of only a few neurons with strong choice-related signals, we examined the classifier weight associated with each neuron. Almost all neuronal weights were non-zero in each session, indicating that every neuron in the population contributed to the classifier's performance (Figure S5A).
evidence: This is shown in Figure S5A plotting classifier weights for all neurons.

claim: Across different modalities, the signal carried by the PPC population closely paralleled behavior.
source: Across different modalities, the signal carried by the PPC population closely paralleled behavior.
evidence: This is shown in Figure 6C comparing decoder psychometric curves to behavioral psychometric curves.

claim: Since for each trial the classifier was trained using only trials with modality different from the test trial, we can conclude that the population code was supramodal and could account for the rats' behavior.
source: Notably, since for each trial the classifier was trained using only trials with modality different from the test trial, we can conclude that the population code was supramodal and could account for the rats' behavior (Figure S5B).
evidence: This conclusion is based on the cross-modality training and testing procedure of the classifier.

claim: Rats were able to perform fine orientation discriminations in the purely visual (V) condition, confirming that the visual capacities of Long-Evans rats might be widely underestimated.
source: Rats were able to perform fine orientation discriminations in the purely visual (V) condition, confirming that the visual capacities of Long-Evans rats might be widely underestimated (Zoccolan, 2015).
evidence: This conclusion is based on the observed visual discrimination performance and supported by citation.

claim: In the purely tactile (T) condition, rats were also able to perform fine orientation discriminations using their snout and whiskers.
source: In the purely tactile (T) condition, rats were also able to perform fine orientation discriminations using their snout and whiskers.
evidence: This is based on the observed tactile discrimination performance.

claim: In an earlier report (Polley et al., 2005), rats learned in a single trial to avoid shock by escaping the arm of a maze containing protruding vertical bars on the wall; horizontal bars marked the safe arms.
source: In an earlier report (Polley et al., 2005), rats learned in a single trial to avoid shock by escaping the arm of a maze containing protruding vertical bars on the wall; horizontal bars marked the safe arms.
evidence: This is a description of previous work cited to provide context.

claim: In the Polley et al. study, orientation detection was whisker dependent, and resolution was on the order of 45°.
source: Orientation detection was whisker dependent, and resolution was on the order of 45°.
evidence: This describes findings from the cited Polley et al., 2005 study.

claim: In both modalities rats were sensitive to 5° angle differences.
source: In our study, in both modalities rats were sensitive to 5° angle differences (e.g., non-overlapping error bars for neighboring angles in Figures 2A and 2B).
evidence: This is demonstrated by the psychometric curves showing discrimination of adjacent 5° steps.

claim: Supralinear behavioral combination of sensory channels has been found in previous studies.
source: Supralinear behavioral combination of sensory channels has been found in previous studies (Fetsch et al., 2009, 2011; Kiani et al., 2013; Raposo et al., 2012).
evidence: This claim is supported by citations to previous studies.

claim: Supralinear combination could occur if the information available on unimodal trials were only partially exploited, while the information available on bimodal trials were completely exploited.
source: It could occur if the information available on unimodal trials were only partially exploited, while the information available on bimodal trials were completely exploited.
evidence: This is a theoretical explanation for supralinear performance cited from previous work.

claim: Partial exploitation of information on unimodal trials might stem from reduced motivation: rats are sensitive to overall reward rate, and unimodal trials yield lower average reward rates than do bimodal trials.
source: Partial exploitation of information on unimodal trials might stem from reduced motivation: rats are sensitive to overall reward rate, and unimodal trials yield lower average reward rates than do bimodal trials.
evidence: This is a hypothetical explanation for potential confounds in multisensory integration studies.

claim: In all modality conditions, rats' response time was nearly 150 ms longer on trials with orientation near the 45° category boundary.
source: In all modality conditions, rats' response time was nearly 150 ms longer on trials with orientation near the 45° category boundary (Figure 2G), indicating that they did not truncate the collection of evidence on more difficult trials.
evidence: This is shown in Figure 2G and argues against premature evidence truncation.

claim: Response time distributions were similar across the three modality conditions, suggesting an attempt to accumulate sensory evidence on unimodal trials just as on bimodal trials.
source: Similarly, response time distributions were similar across the three modality conditions (Figure S2F), suggesting an attempt to accumulate sensory evidence on unimodal trials just as on bimodal trials.
evidence: This is shown in Figure S2F and argues against differential motivation across modalities.

claim: Comparing across sessions, rats still combined V and T signals in a supralinear fashion in block design sessions.
source: Yet, comparing across sessions, rats still combined V and T signals in a supralinear fashion (Figure 2F).
evidence: This is shown in Figure 2F comparing block design to interleaved design.

claim: Both cue-combination models employed in our study (Bayesian decision theory and MI) assume independence of the sensory channels.
source: Both cue-combination models employed in our study (Bayesian decision theory and MI) assume independence of the sensory channels.
evidence: This describes an assumption of the analytical models used.

claim: The observed supralinearity could result from a violation of the independence assumption—visual and tactile signals might not be processed independently in bimodal trials.
source: The observed supralinearity could result from a violation of that assumption—visual and tactile signals might not be processed independently in bimodal trials.
evidence: This is a proposed explanation for the observed supralinear performance.

claim: The presence of one signal might affect how the other is acquired by the sensorimotor system.
source: The presence of one signal might affect how the other is acquired by the sensorimotor system.
evidence: This is a hypothetical mechanism for cross-modal interaction.

claim: The visual cue might help the rat palpate the surface with its whiskers more efficiently, boosting the tactile signal.
source: For instance, the visual cue might help the rat palpate the surface with its whiskers more efficiently, boosting the tactile signal (Prescott et al., 2011).
evidence: This is a specific hypothetical mechanism supported by citation.

claim: A second possible form of interaction is synergy between the two processing pathways within cortex.
source: A second possible form of interaction is synergy between the two processing pathways within cortex.
evidence: This is a proposed alternative mechanism for supralinear performance.

claim: Inhibition between cortical regions could allow activity evoked through one modality to suppress the non-specific noise in the other modality's stimulus representation.
source: Inhibition between cortical regions could allow activity evoked through one modality to suppress the non-specific noise in the other modality's stimulus representation.
evidence: This is a hypothetical mechanism for cross-modal cortical interaction.

claim: In the mouse, activation of auditory cortex by a noise burst drives local GABAergic inhibition in the primary visual cortex, via cortico-cortical connections.
source: Evidence of direct functional connections between sensory cortical processing regions is emerging. In the mouse, activation of auditory cortex by a noise burst drives local GABAergic inhibition in the primary visual cortex, via cortico-cortical connections (Iurilli et al., 2012).
evidence: This is evidence from a cited study supporting the possibility of cross-modal cortical interactions.

claim: Brief pulses of auditory noise sharpen orientation selectivity of L2/L3 pyramidal neurons in primary visual cortex.
source: Similarly, brief pulses of auditory noise sharpen orientation selectivity of L2/L3 pyramidal neurons in primary visual cortex (Ibrahim et al., 2016).
evidence: This is evidence from a cited study supporting cross-modal enhancement of sensory processing.

claim: Primate PPC receives input from cortical areas representing multiple sensory modalities and communicates back to these territories.
source: Primate PPC receives input from cortical areas representing multiple sensory modalities and communicates back to these territories (Cavada and Goldman-Rakic, 1989; Leichnetz, 2001).
evidence: This anatomical claim about primate PPC is supported by citations.

claim: PPC in rats is held to be homologous to primate PPC, as it shares the characteristic reciprocal connectivity with sensory, frontal, temporal, and limbic cortical regions.
source: PPC in rats is held to be homologous to primate PPC, as it shares the characteristic reciprocal connectivity with sensory, frontal, temporal, and limbic cortical regions (Akers and Killackey, 1978; Miller and Vogt, 1984; Reep et al., 1994; Whitlock et al., 2008; Wilber et al., 2014).
evidence: This claim about rat-primate homology is supported by multiple anatomical citations.

claim: PPC is well positioned to receive unimodal sensory signals and transmit a processed form of those signals to downstream cortical regions.
source: It is thus well positioned to receive unimodal sensory signals (Kolb and Walkey, 1987; Olcese et al., 2013; Reep et al., 1994) and transmit a processed form of those signals to downstream cortical regions.
evidence: This functional interpretation is based on anatomical connectivity supported by citations.

claim: Each neuron's firing rates were the same under the V, T, and VT conditions.
source: Each neuron's firing rates were the same under the V, T, and VT conditions.
evidence: This is demonstrated by the correlation analysis in Figure 4.

claim: The orientation and category signal carried by neurons in the three modality conditions could not be distinguished except by its greater robustness in VT trials—mirroring the greater behavioral robustness in VT trials.
source: Indeed, the orientation and category signal carried by neurons in the three modality conditions could not be distinguished except by its greater robustness in VT trials—mirroring the greater behavioral robustness in VT trials (Figure 6C).
evidence: This is shown in Figure 6C comparing decoder performance across modalities.

claim: An individual neuron could show graded steps in firing rate within an orientation category (thus, stronger angle tuning) or else a large step in firing rate around 45° (thus, stronger category tuning).
source: Since orientation and perceived category were correlated, the two forms of coding were distributed along a single dimension, such that an individual neuron could show graded steps in firing rate within an orientation category (thus, stronger angle tuning) or else a large step in firing rate around 45° (thus, stronger category tuning).
evidence: This describes the continuum of neuronal tuning properties observed in PPC.

claim: When tested, the classifier replicated the rat's behavior on over 90% of trials.
source: When tested, the classifier replicated the rat's behavior on over 90% of trials (Figure 6B).
evidence: This is the result of linear discriminant analysis on population activity.

claim: PPC contributes to the formation of a supramodal representation of the object and participates in the percept-to-action transformation carried out by a network of cortical regions.
source: Considering together the two key properties, we suggest that PPC contributes to the formation of a supramodal representation of the object and participates in the percept-to-action transformation carried out by a network of cortical regions.
evidence: This is the main conclusion drawn from the combined behavioral and neurophysiological findings.

claim: This interpretation is based on correlations between trial-by-trial neuronal firing and whole-animal behavior; evidence for a direct causal role of PPC might emerge from external manipulations of this region during execution of the behavior.
source: This interpretation is based on correlations between trial-by-trial neuronal firing and whole-animal behavior; evidence for a direct causal role of PPC might emerge from external manipulations of this region during execution of the behavior.
evidence: This acknowledges the correlational nature of the findings and suggests future experiments.

claim: While PPC could receive orientation-tuned signals from earlier stages of visual cortical processing, tactile orientation tuning has not been reported in barrel cortex and may be generated in PPC to meet specific behavioral demands.
source: While PPC could receive orientation-tuned signals from earlier stages of visual cortical processing (Wang et al., 2012), tactile orientation tuning has not been reported in barrel cortex and may be generated in PPC to meet specific behavioral demands.
evidence: This interpretation combines citation about visual processing with inference about tactile processing.

claim: The reward rule boundary of 45° was set arbitrarily, and its neuronal correlate must also be generated to meet behavioral demands.
source: Furthermore, the reward rule boundary of 45° was set arbitrarily, and its neuronal correlate must also be generated to meet behavioral demands.
evidence: This is an inference based on the arbitrary nature of the task design.

claim: These observations lead to the conclusion that the functional properties of PPC neurons are not hardwired but rather emerge through a learning process in order to solve the requirements of ongoing tasks.
source: These observations lead to the conclusion that the functional properties of PPC neurons are not hardwired but rather emerge through a learning process in order to solve the requirements of ongoing tasks.
evidence: This conclusion is based on the task-specific nature of the observed neuronal properties.

claim: Numerous studies have trained subjects to associate the sensory data originating in two modalities by some arbitrary rule.
source: Numerous studies have trained subjects to associate the sensory data originating in two modalities by some arbitrary rule (Sarko et al., 2013).
evidence: This describes previous multisensory integration studies supported by citation.

claim: In a recent study in which rats judged the quantities of auditory and/or visual pulses in a train, many neurons in PPC responded differentially on trials of the two modalities; indeed, modality itself could be decoded from neuronal firing.
source: In a recent study in which rats judged the quantities of auditory and/or visual pulses in a train, many neurons in PPC responded differentially on trials of the two modalities; indeed, modality itself could be decoded from neuronal firing (Raposo et al., 2014).
evidence: This describes findings from a cited study that contrast with the current findings.

claim: This contrasts with the modality-free coding of the object in our study.
source: This contrasts with the modality-free coding of the object in our study.
evidence: This explicitly contrasts the current findings with those of Raposo et al., 2014.

claim: We hypothesize that supramodal coding is a consequence of the nearly simultaneous arrival in PPC of congruous signals, through two sensory channels, about a real object.
source: We hypothesize that supramodal coding is a consequence of the nearly simultaneous arrival in PPC of congruous signals, through two sensory channels, about a real object.
evidence: This is a hypothesis proposed to explain the difference between studies.

claim: Coherence between modalities, as in our study, might better reflect the statistics of the real world.
source: Coherence between modalities, as in our study, might better reflect the statistics of the real world.
evidence: This is a speculative statement about ecological validity.

claim: Vision and touch have likely evolved together to explore the shape, form, and spatial properties of the environment.
source: Vision and touch have likely evolved together to explore the shape, form, and spatial properties of the environment.
evidence: This is a general statement about sensory evolution.

claim: A rodent might need to maneuver through oriented bars, whether those bars are seen or felt.
source: A rodent might need to maneuver through oriented bars, whether those bars are seen or felt.
evidence: This is a speculative statement about ecological relevance.

claim: The brain's capacity to call up knowledge about things independently of sensory input channel requires a stage of processing in which the same information is encoded by both channels, and PPC seems to contribute to this abstraction of stimuli from sensory domains.
source: In general, the brain's capacity to call up knowledge about things independently of sensory input channel (Quiroga et al., 2005) requires a stage of processing in which the same information is encoded by both channels, and PPC seems to contribute to this abstraction of stimuli from sensory domains.
evidence: This is the main theoretical conclusion combining citation with current findings.

claim: Twelve male Long–Evans rats were used in the study.
source: Twelve male Long–Evans rats (Charles River Laboratories, Calco, Italy) were used.
evidence: This describes the experimental subjects.

claim: Rats were maintained on a 12/12 hr light/dark cycle; experiments were conducted during the light phase.
source: They were maintained on a 12/12 hr light/dark cycle; experiments were conducted during the light phase.
evidence: This describes experimental conditions.

claim: Upon arrival rats were 8 weeks old, weighing approximately 250 g, and typically grew to over 600 g over the course of the study.
source: Upon arrival they were 8 weeks old, weighing approximately 250 g, and typically grew to over 600 g over the course of the study.
evidence: This describes the age and weight of experimental subjects.

claim: During each session rats received 17-22 mL of pear juice diluted in water (1 unit juice: 4 units water) as reward.
source: During each session they received 17-22 mL of pear juice diluted in water (1 unit juice: 4 units water) as reward.
evidence: This describes the reward used in the behavioral task.

claim: Protocols conformed to international norms and were approved by the Ethics Committee of SISSA and by the Italian Health Ministry (license numbers 569/2015-PR and 570/2015-PR).
source: Protocols conformed to international norms and were approved by the Ethics Committee of SISSA and by the Italian Health Ministry (license numbers 569/2015-PR and 570/2015-PR).
evidence: This describes ethical approval for the study.
